name: Coleridge-Taylor Perkinson
abstract: Coleridge-Taylor Perkinson (June 14, 1932, Manhattan, New York or possibly (unconfirmed)  Winston-Salem, North Carolina – March 9, 2004, Chicago) was an American composer whose interests spanned the worlds of jazz, dance, pop, film, television, and classical music. Professionally he was often known as "Coleridge Perkinson".

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: later on, Coleridge-Taylor Perkinson, who is a very dear friend
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses the album 'A New Perspective' featuring Coleridge Perkinson but does not mention any personal interaction, collaboration, or relationship with Perkinson himself, only with Donald Byrd and others.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ruth Lion
about_quotes.evidence: Wonderful music with Coleridge Perkinson as the one who took care of the voices, and that was 18 vocals and the musicians.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ruth Lion interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: Coleridge-Taylor Perkinson was going to arrange the music, and he had said, “I want Jimmy Owens in the trumpet section.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Branford Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: You know, you can’t put Mozart, Coleridge Taylor Perkinson. It doesn’t work. You can’t juxtapose those two.
about_quotes.doc_title: Branford Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
